quiz.title: Unit 3 Quiz 2: Stagflation, Inflation Types, Expectations, and Policy
quiz.subtitle: Cost-Push vs Demand-Pull Inflation, Rational Expectations, and AD-AS Adjustments
quiz.subject: Macroeconomics
quiz.topic: First Year - Stagflation, Cost-Push Inflation, Demand-Pull Inflation, Rational Expectations

questions.question: The combination of a rising _____ and decreasing _____ is called stagflation.
questions.explanation: Stagflation is the combination of rising prices (inflation) and falling output (real GDP decreases), which also means rising unemployment.

questions.question: An inflation that is kicked off by an increase in _____ is called cost-push inflation.
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation starts with an increase in production costs (wages, raw materials), which shifts aggregate supply left.

questions.question: An inflation that starts because _____ is called demand-pull inflation.
questions.explanation: Demand-pull inflation occurs when aggregate demand increases faster than aggregate supply, pulling prices up.

questions.question: A rational expectation is a forecast that results from the use of all the relevant data and _____.
questions.explanation: Rational expectations are formed using all available relevant data and economic theory (economic science).

questions.question: Starting at full employment, the price of oil falls unexpectedly and aggregate supply increases by $70 billion. Select all that apply.
options: Workers may ask for higher wages
answer: Workers may ask for higher wages
questions.explanation: A positive supply shock (lower oil prices) shifts AS right. Real GDP rises above potential (inflationary gap), unemployment falls below natural rate. Eventually workers demand higher wages as prices adjust.

questions.question: If the Central bank responds to an overheated economy using contractionary measures, this may lead to __________. (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
questions.explanation: Contractionary policy decreases AD. This reduces inflationary pressure, leading to disinflation (falling inflation rate) caused by demand-side forces — demand-pull disinflation.

questions.question: An economy at a full-employment equilibrium experiences an increase in aggregate demand. The unemployment rate ______ its natural rate, and to return to the long-run equilibrium, the money wage rate begins to ______.
questions.explanation: An increase in AD raises output above potential, so unemployment falls below the natural rate. To return to long-run equilibrium, money wages rise, shifting SRAS left.

questions.question: A cost-push rise in the price level can arise from an increase in _______. SELECT ALL THAT APPLY
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation comes from increases in production costs — higher wages or higher raw material prices. Government expenditure, exports, and money supply affect demand, not costs directly.

questions.question: An economy is at potential GDP when it experiences an increase in costs. The economy experiences _______.
options: a rise in the price level and an increase in real GDP
a rise in the price level and a decrease in potential GDP
a fall in the price level and a decrease in real GDP
questions.explanation: An increase in costs shifts AS left. Prices rise and real GDP falls below potential. This combination of rising prices and falling output is stagflation.

questions.question: A stagflation can turn into a cost-push inflation process when _______.
options: the quantity of money persistently decreases
the quantity of money persistently increases
questions.explanation: If the central bank persistently increases the money supply to fight unemployment caused by stagflation, it accommodates cost-push pressures, leading to a continued cost-push inflation spiral.

questions.question: The best forecast available, which is based on all the relevant information is called a _______.
questions.explanation: A rational expectation is a forecast made using all available relevant data and economic theory. It may still be wrong, but it's the best possible given available information.

questions.question: If the government increases its expenditure on goods and services and as a result, the money wage rate increases, the economy has experienced _______.
options: a demand-pull rise in the price level
questions.explanation: The initial increase in government spending increases AD (demand-pull). The subsequent wage increase is a secondary effect, not the initial cause. The inflation started with demand.

questions.question: Deflation is a _______.
options: one-time fall in the price level
questions.explanation: Deflation is a sustained, persistent decrease in the overall price level, not just a one-time drop.

questions.question: Under rational expectations, if the central bank announces a credible policy to reduce inflation, the adjustment to the new lower inflation rate will likely be _______.
options: very slow, with high unemployment for years
rapid, with little change in output
only possible if the money supply increases
questions.explanation: With rational expectations, if policy is credible, people immediately adjust their inflation expectations. Wages and prices adjust quickly, so output falls only briefly or not at all.

questions.question: Which of the following are likely to cause demand-pull inflation? SELECT ALL THAT APPLY.
options: A sharp increase in oil prices
A large tax cut for households
An increase in government infrastructure spending
A rise in the money supply faster than output growth
answer: A large tax cut for households
An increase in government infrastructure spending
A rise in the money supply faster than output growth
questions.explanation: Tax cuts, higher government spending, and rapid money growth all increase aggregate demand, pulling prices up. Oil price increases and droughts affect supply, not demand.

questions.question: During the 1970s oil price shocks, many economies experienced both rising unemployment and rising inflation. This is an example of _______.
questions.explanation: Higher oil prices increase production costs (cost-push), shifting AS left. The result is higher prices and lower output (higher unemployment) — stagflation.

questions.question: If people form their inflation expectations adaptively, a one-time increase in the money supply will cause inflation to _______.
options: rise temporarily, then fall back to zero
rise and stay high until people see lower inflation
questions.explanation: With adaptive expectations, people base future expectations on past inflation. After a monetary expansion, inflation rises and persists until people observe lower inflation over time, which can take years.

questions.question: Which of the following are likely outcomes of a negative supply shock (e.g., a spike in global commodity prices) in the short run? SELECT ALL THAT APPLY.
questions.explanation: A negative supply shock shifts AS left, raising prices, lowering real GDP, and increasing unemployment. Potential GDP is unchanged unless the shock permanently affects productive capacity.

questions.question: Under rational expectations, systematic monetary policy that is fully anticipated by the public will have _______ effect on real GDP in the long run.
questions.explanation: Rational expectations imply that anticipated policy changes are already factored into wages and prices. Monetary policy is neutral in the long run, affecting only prices, not real GDP.

questions.question: A wage-price spiral is most closely associated with _______.
questions.explanation: A wage-price spiral occurs when higher prices lead to higher wage demands, which raise costs further, shifting AS left again — a persistent cost-push process, especially if the central bank accommodates it.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You understand stagflation, cost-push vs demand-pull inflation, and rational expectations.
scoreMessages.70: Good work. Review the difference between supply shocks (cost-push) and demand shocks (demand-pull).
scoreMessages.50: Solid effort. Focus on what triggers each type of inflation and how expectations work.
scoreMessages.0: Keep studying. Remember: Cost-push starts with costs (supply). Demand-pull starts with demand. Stagflation = rising prices + falling output.
